result:
If I could build a sanctuary, it would be a room made entirely of windows. Not for the view—though that would be splendid—but for the light. Morning light, the kind that arrives hesitantly, painting the world in pale gold and long shadows. Noon light, bold and demanding, revealing every detail without mercy. And evening light, the most forgiving, turning everything soft and gilded before it slips away.

We spend so much time in manufactured light, under the flat, constant glow of LEDs, that we forget how sunlight moves. It has moods. It changes the colour of a wall, the texture of a book page, the very air in a room. In my window-room, time wouldn’t be measured by clocks, but by these shifting qualities of light. There would be no artificial bulbs; when dusk finally came, the room would surrender to darkness, and you’d know the day was truly over.

I think such a place would teach quiet lessons. About transience—how each perfect, illuminated moment passes, making way for the next. About clarity—what it means to see things starkly, then softly, then not at all. And about resilience—how the light returns, without fail, every single morning, no matter how deep the previous night.

Perhaps we all need a little more of this: a space where we are not illuminated, but *illuminated upon*. Where we are passive recipients of an ancient, graceful cycle, rather than active controllers of our environment. A place to simply sit, and be painted by the day.

There would be no artificial bulbs; when dusk finally came, the room would surrender to darkness, and you’d know the day was truly over.
